questions.question: Why is pixel-space diffusion at 512² prohibitively expensive?
options: GPUs can't hold 512x512 tensors
Each sampling step is ~100 GFLOPS for a 500M U-Net, 50 steps is 5 TFLOPS per image — and most FLOPs go to perceptually unimportant high-frequency texture a lossy VAE could compress away
Pixel data can't be noised properly
512² requires 1000+ sampling steps
questions.explanation: Rombach's 2022 insight: you don't need all 786k dimensions for semantic structure. Run diffusion in a VAE's 64×64×4 latent space — 1/16th the values, ~64x fewer FLOPs, comparable quality. That IS Stable Diffusion.

questions.question: What are latent diffusion's two separately trained stages?
options: A generator then a discriminator

Stage 1: a VAE (8x spatial downsample, L1 + LPIPS + light KL + often adversarial loss for sharp decodes), trained once and frozen; stage 2: standard diffusion treating z = E(x) as the data, decoding the sampled z₀ back to pixels
Two diffusion models at different sizes
Pretraining then RLHF
questions.explanation: The KL weight is kept small because z doesn't need to be exactly Gaussian — diffusion will handle sampling. The diffusion loss itself is identical to plain DDPM; only the data domain changes.

questions.question: How does text get into the image during generation?
options: The prompt is rendered as pixels first

A frozen text encoder produces tokens, and every U-Net/DiT block runs cross-attention with Q = image features and K = V = text tokens — those tokens are the ONLY channel through which text influences the image
Text is concatenated to the latent
A separate captioning model verifies output
questions.explanation: Encoder choice matters: CLIP-L for SD 1.x, dual CLIP for SDXL, and T5-XXL for SD3/Flux — T5 beats CLIP for prompt adherence, which is why newer models carry a full language encoder.

questions.question: What are the three architecture trends from SD 1.5 to Flux?
options: Smaller models, fewer tokens, less data
Replace the U-Net with a DiT (transformer over latent patches), scale up the text encoder (T5-XXL), and increase latent channels from 4 to 16 for more detail headroom
Move from latents back to pixels
Drop text conditioning for image prompts
questions.explanation: SD3 made the switch to MMDiT with flow matching; Flux.1-dev scales it to 12B. Turbo/schnell variants distill to 1-4 sampling steps for real-time use.

questions.question: Why does the same diffusion code work unchanged on latents?
options: Latents are secretly pixels

Diffusion only assumes its data is a continuous tensor it can noise and denoise — it doesn't care whether values are raw pixels or VAE encodings, which is why the toy lesson can swap in encode/decode and rerun the identical DDPM loss
The VAE retrains the U-Net automatically
It doesn't — latents need a new loss
questions.explanation: That domain-independence is the deep point of the two-stage recipe: the expensive perceptual work lives in the frozen VAE, and the generative work happens wherever it's cheapest.
